text.title: DDD Value Objects Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding the Value Object pattern.

rule.statement: Value Objects MUST be immutable by the type system. Python: `@dataclass(frozen=True)`. Java/Kotlin: `record`/`data class` with `val` only. C#: `record` or `readonly record struct`. Mutation via attribute assignment MUST throw at runtime.
rule.rationale: Conventional immutability fails under careless refactor; type-level immutability is enforced by the compiler/runtime.

rule.statement: Validation MUST happen in the constructor (`__post_init__` for `@dataclass`, constructor body for Java/C#). An invalid Value Object MUST NOT be constructible — invalid input raises/throws immediately.

rationale.source: DDD Reference — Make invalid states unrepresentable

rule.rationale: Validating after construction allows callers to hold invalid instances; constructor validation pushes the error to the boundary.

rule.statement: Equality MUST be by attributes, not by identity. Two Value Objects with the same field values MUST compare equal (`__eq__` / `equals` / `record` default).
rule.rationale: Identity equality on Value Objects breaks set/dict semantics and confuses domain reasoning ("two Money(10, USD) should be the same Money").

rule.statement: Operations that produce a new value MUST return a new instance. `Money.add(other)` returns `Money(self.amount + other.amount, self.currency)`. Mutating `self.amount` is forbidden by rule #1; even constructing in place is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Returning new instances makes Value Objects safe to share across threads + caches; mutation breaks both.

rule.statement: Value Objects MUST NOT carry an identity field (`id`, `uuid`). If a concept needs identity, it's an Entity, not a Value Object — use `[[ddd-aggregates]]` instead.

rationale.source: DDD Reference — Entities vs Value Objects

rule.rationale: Mixing identity into a value type produces equality bugs: two "same" addresses with different IDs compare unequal; collections deduplicate incorrectly.
